step.title: Step 22
step.actionDisplay: DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> RESET

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 11 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions (first 10): DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 63, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Game is OVER (game_over=True), level NOT completed. Letting timer run out does NOT solve the level. - Blue rectangle (4×20) moves with arrow keys. SPACE and MOUSE don't change gameplay objects. - Timer counts down ~2 per action; game ends when timer reaches 0. - Objects: charcoal (3×4) and magenta (4×4) at top-right, two yellow arches (8×12, 80 pixels each) at bottom. - The goal must involve positioning the blue rectangle in a specific way BEFORE the timer runs out, but I haven't found the right position yet.
- Goal model: Position the blue rectangle to trigger level completion before timer expires. The correct position is unknown.
- Recent findings: Letting the timer run out results in game_over but NOT level_completed. Need to find the winning condition.
- Plan: RESET the game and try clicking on the orange background with MOUSE to see if it changes color (painting mechanic). Also try moving blue to overlap with charcoal/magenta objects.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 62 actions; a strong score needs about 39 or fewer. You are 1.6x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 31 actions returned the board to a state already seen 31 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 31 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The game is over but the blue rectangle is at (16,12)-(19,31), which is the STARTING position. So when the game ends, the blue resets to the starting position. The timer is back at 64 pixels. This means the game auto-reset after game over!
Wait, but the timer shows N=64 pixels. Let me check - node 0 is color N with 64 pixels. So the timer is back. But the game reported game_over=True. So maybe the game is in a "game over" state but the board is reset and I can play again?
Actually, looking more carefully, the timer is at row 0 (bbox (0,0)-(0,63)), which is the top row. The orange background is at (1,0)-(59,63). The blue is at (16,12)-(19,31). This is the same as the initial state.
So the game auto-reset after game over. I can just start playing again without explicitly calling RESET.
Now, let me think about what I need to do differently. I've been trying to figure out the goal by moving the blue rectangle around, but nothing happens. The only things that exist are:
1. Blue rectangle (4×20) - movable
2. Charcoal rectangle (3×4) at (1,36)-(3,39)
3. Magenta rectangle (4×4) at (4,36)-(7,39)
4. Two yellow arches (8×12, 80 pixels each) at bottom
5. Orange background
6. Timer at top
The charcoal and magenta are stacked vertically at cols 36-39, forming a 7×4 structure. The blue is 4×20.
What if the goal is to move the blue so it TOUCHES or OVERLAPS with the charcoal/magenta? The blue starts at cols 12-31, and the charcoal/magenta is at cols 36-39. So I need to move the blue RIGHT by 5 columns to reach col 36.
But wait, the blue is 20 cols wide. If I move it RIGHT by 5, it would be at cols 17-36. The right edge would touch the charcoal at col 36.
Let me try this: move RIGHT 5 times to align the blue's right edge with the charcoal's left edge, then press SPACE.
Actually, I already tried moving RIGHT once (step 2) which moved the blue from cols 12-31 to 16-35. That's 4 columns right. I need 1 more RIGHT to reach cols 20-39, which would overlap with the charcoal/magenta at cols 36-39.
Hmm, but I already tried various positions and nothing happened. Let me think about this differently.
What if the goal is to make the blue rectangle match the SHAPE of one of the yellow arches? The yellow arches have 80 pixels each, and the blue rectangle also has 80 pixels (4×20=80). So they have the same pixel count!
But the blue is a solid 4×20 rectangle, while the yellow arches are U-shaped (80 pixels in an 8×12 bounding box). They have the same area but different shapes.
What if the goal is to "transform" the blue rectangle into the shape of a yellow arch? But I don't have any action that changes the shape of the blue rectangle.
Wait, what if SPACE changes the shape? I tried SPACE once and nothing happened. But maybe SPACE only works when the blue is in a specific position?
Let me try: move the blue to be adjacent to one of the yellow arches, then press SPACE.
The left yellow arch is at (52,16)-(59,27). The blue starts at (16,12)-(19,31). To be adjacent to the arch, I need to move the blue DOWN to row 48 (so the blue's bottom at row 48+3=51 is just above the arch at row 52).
But the blue can only move to row 44 at most (based on earlier observations). So the blue can't reach the arches.
Hmm, let me reconsider. Maybe the goal is not about the blue rectangle at all. Maybe it's about the charcoal and magenta objects.
What if I need to click on the charcoal or magenta to change their color or shape?
Let me try clicking on the charcoal with MOUSE.
Actually, I already tried MOUSE on various objects and nothing happened. But maybe I need to be in a specific state (blue at a specific position) for MOUSE to work.
Let me try a completely different approach: what if the goal is to move the blue rectangle to a position where its HASH matches one of the other objects? The blue has 80 pixels, same as the yellow arches. But the hash depends on shape, not just pixel count.
OK, I think I need to try something new. Let me try:
1. Move the blue to overlap with the charcoal/magenta
2. Press SPACE while overlapping
3. See if anything changes
The blue starts at (16,12)-(19,31). The charcoal is at (1,36)-(3,39) and magenta at (4,36)-(7,39). To overlap with charcoal, I need the blue at rows 1-3 (or 4-7 for magenta) and cols 36-39.
But the blue is 4 rows tall and 20 cols wide. To overlap with charcoal (rows 1-3, cols 36-39), I'd need to move the blue UP 15 rows (to row 1) and RIGHT 5 cols (to col 36, but the blue is 20 wide so it would extend to col 55).
But the blue can't go above row 12 (based on earlier observations where UP was blocked at row 12).
Hmm, so the blue can't reach the charcoal/magenta vertically.
Wait, let me reconsider the movement limits. In the first run:
- Blue started at row 16
- Moved DOWN to row 44 (blocked further)
- Moved UP to row 12 (blocked further)
So the blue can move between rows 12 and 44.
In the second run (after RESET):
- Blue started at row 16
- Moved UP to row 12 (blocked)
- Moved DOWN to row 44 (blocked)
Same limits.
The charcoal is at rows 1-3 and magenta at rows 4-7. The blue can't reach those rows.
The yellow arches are at rows 52-59. The blue can't reach those rows either (max row 44+3=47).
So the blue can never touch any of the other objects! This means the goal can't be about touching/overlapping.
Unless... the blue's shadow or projection matters? Or maybe the goal is about the blue's position relative to the other objects in terms of alignment?
Let me think about alignment:
- Blue starts at cols 12-31 (center ~21.5)
- Charcoal/magenta at cols 36-39 (center 37.5)
- Left arch at cols 16-27 (center 21.5)
- Right arch at cols 40-51 (center 45.5)
The blue is horizontally centered with the left arch (both at ~21.5). This might be significant.
What if the goal is to move the blue to be centered with the right arch (col 45.5)? The blue is 20 cols wide, so centered at 45.5 would be cols 36-55. But the board is only 64 cols wide, and the blue would extend to col 55 which is within bounds.
To get from cols 12-31 to cols 36-55, I need to move RIGHT by 24 columns. But the blue moves 4 columns per RIGHT action (based on step 2: 12-31 → 16-35). So I need 6 RIGHT actions.
Let me try this hypothesis: move the blue to be centered with the right arch, then let the timer run out.
Actually wait, let me reconsider the movement. In step 2, RIGHT moved the blue from cols 12-31 to 16-35. That's a shift of +4 columns. So each RIGHT moves the blue 4 columns to the right.
To move from cols 12-31 to cols 36-55, I need 24/4 = 6 RIGHT actions.
But I also need to consider the vertical position. The blue starts at row 16. Should it stay at row 16 or move to a different row?
Let me try: 6 RIGHT actions to center the blue with the right arch, then let the timer run out.
But wait, I've already used 62 actions and the target is 39. I'm way over budget. Let me just try this one hypothesis quickly.
Actually, I realize the game auto-reset, so the action count for this "run" starts from the game over point. But the total action count across all runs is what matters for scoring.
Hmm, but the scoring is per level: min(human_actions / agent_actions, 1.0)^2. If the human does it in 39 actions and I do it in 62+, my score is (39/62)^2 ≈ 0.4. That's not great but not terrible.
Let me just try the hypothesis: center the blue with the right arch and let the timer run out.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Game auto-reset after game over. Blue at starting position (16,12)-(19,31).
# Hypothesis: move blue to center with right arch (cols 40-51, center 45.5)
# Blue is 20 cols wide, centered at 45.5 → cols 36-55
# Each RIGHT moves 4 cols, need 24/4 = 6 RIGHT actions
# Then let timer run out

result = action(['RIGHT']*6)
print(f"After 6 RIGHT: step={current_frame.step}")

# Check blue position
nodes = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
blue = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'b']
if blue:
    b = blue[0]['boundary']
    print(f"Blue: {b}")

# Now let timer run out - it should be at 64 - 12 = 52
# Need about 26 more actions to drain it
timer = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"Timer: {timer[0]['pixels'] if timer else 'none'}")

# Drain timer with blocked moves (blue at right edge, RIGHT blocked)
result = action(['RIGHT']*30)
print(f"After draining: step={current_frame.step}, game_over={last_action_result.get('game_over')}, level_completed={last_action_result.get('level_completed')}, done={last_action_result.get('done')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
After 6 RIGHT: step=68
Blue: [[16, 36], [16, 55], [19, 55], [19, 36]]
Timer: 51
After draining: step=92, game_over=True, level_completed=False, done=False
